answer:
**The work is *Old English Elegies* (slug: `unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e`), chapter 13 ("Welund him be wurman wraeces cunnade"), which contains the Old English poem known as *Deor*.**[^1]

This chapter features a stanzaic catalogue of legendary misfortunes—Weland (Wayland the Smith) bound by Niðhad's sinew-fetters,[^2] Beadohild's unwilling pregnancy,[^3] the boundless sorrow-love between Mæðhild and Geat,[^4] Theodric's thirty winters in the Mærings' stronghold,[^5] and Eormanric's wolfish rule over the Goths[^6]—with each stanza closing on the refrain **"That passed over; so may this."** The speaker, a scop (poet) displaced from his lord's favor by a rival named Heorrenda, finally names himself **"Deor"** in the closing stanza.[^7]

Three occurrences of the refrain (from the Weland, Mæðhilde, and Deor stanzas):

> Weland knew well the worm’s cruel bite,  
> a resolute earl endured his hardships,  
> had for his company sorrow and longing,  
> winter-cold misery; woes he often met  
> after Niðhad had laid heavy needs on him,  
> supple sinew-bonds on a better man.  
> **That passed over; so may this.**[^2]

> We have heard tell of Mæðhilde's plight,  
> how the love of Geat grew boundless,  
> so that sorrow-love stole all their sleep.  
> **That passed over; so may this.**[^4]

> This I can say about my own self,  
> that for a time I was the Heodenings' scop,  
> dear to my lord. **Deor was my name.**  
> For many winters I had a goodly following,  
> a loyal lord, until Heorrenda now,  
> that song-crafty man, received the land-right  
> which the protector of earls had granted me before.  
> **That passed over; so may this.**[^7]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, chapter 13, [full chapter](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/).

[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, chapter 13, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-1308a0).

[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, chapter 13, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-d11ebc).

[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, chapter 13, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-5821a4).

[^5]: *Old English Elegies*, chapter 13, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-ac23ab).

[^6]: *Old English Elegies*, chapter 13, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-160f1c).

[^7]: *Old English Elegies*, chapter 13, [paragraph](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-6b7e51).
